basmala: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.
intro: Al-Anbiyāʾ — “The Prophets” — opens on an alarm: “the reckoning of mankind draws near, yet in their heedlessness they turn away.” Qushayrī at once distinguishes two heedlessnesses there: that of one who is absent to the world through passion, the mark of separation, and that of one who is absent to all through absorption in his Master, the attribute of union. The sūra then unrolls the great procession of the messengers: Abraham who shatters the idols and whom the fire, by the divine command, receives as “coolness and peace” — for, says Qushayrī, when his heart was pure of all but God, the blaze became for the friend coolness and safety; Job whose patience is such that it was not he who complained of the trial, but the trial that, at its limit, cried out beneath his patience; Jonah who, in the threefold darkness of the night, the sea, and the fish's belly, casts the cry of the shipwrecked: “there is no god but You, glory be to You, I was among the wrongdoers”; Zachariah and Mary, David and Solomon. All culminates in the seal: “We have sent you only as a mercy to the worlds” — raḥmatan li-l-ʿālamīn —, and in the final recourse to the Raḥmān, whose Name that opened the basmala comes to close the sūra.
themes: Abraham and the fire made coolness
the patience of Job, the cry of Jonah
oneness and the return to God

versets.ar: اقْتَرَبَ لِلنَّاسِ حِسَابُهُمْ وَهُمْ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مُّعْرِضُونَ
versets.tr: iqtaraba lin-nāsi ḥisābuhum wa-hum fī ghaflatin muʿriḍūna
versets.trad: The reckoning of mankind draws near, yet in their heedlessness they turn away.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī distinguishes two heedlessnesses: one who forgets his reckoning because he is engulfed in his lower world and his passion, and one who forgets it because he is consumed in his Master. The first is the mark of separation, the second the attribute of union. And he adds: the first sort awaken from their heedlessness only at the intoxication of death; the second never return from their absence, for they are annihilated in the existence of the Real. The same “ghafla” divides or unites, according to whether one is absent to the world or to God.

mots.racine: ق ر ب
ح س ب
غ ف ل / ع ر ض
mots.def: ghafla: heedlessness; muʿriḍ: one who turns away.

versets.ar: مَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن ذِكْرٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِم مُّحْدَثٍ إِلَّا اسْتَمَعُوهُ وَهُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ
versets.tr: mā yaʾtīhim min dhikrin min rabbihim muḥdathin illā stamaʿūhu wa-hum yalʿabūna
versets.trad: No fresh reminder comes to them from their Lord but they listen to it while they play.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We have renewed no messenger to them but they increased in repulsion, nor sent down any discourse but they pushed it back in denial and falsehood; We added no clarification but they took it for jest, nor renewed any blessing but they did what calls for punishment. Thus what We honored them with became a trial for them: such is the conduct of one who behaves badly toward God and loses his due with Him.

mots.racine: ذ ك ر / ح د ث
س م ع / ل ع ب

versets.ar: لَاهِيَةً قُلُوبُهُمْ ۗ وَأَسَرُّوا النَّجْوَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا هَلْ هَٰذَا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ ۖ أَفَتَأْتُونَ السِّحْرَ وَأَنتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ
versets.tr: lāhiyatan qulūbuhum wa-asarrū n-najwā lladhīna ẓalamū hal hādhā illā basharun mithlukum a-fa-taʾtūna s-siḥra wa-antum tubṣirūna
versets.trad: Their hearts are distracted. The wrongdoers confer in secret: “Is this anything but a mortal like yourselves? Will you take to sorcery with your eyes open?”
themes: the secret counsel of the wrongdoers
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: their inner perceptions went blind, their understandings clouded; they are in a dullness that no longer sees clearly, beneath a covering that hides from them the proof set up for them. As for their counsels: unable to answer the challenge, defeated when the Prophet's argument broke over them, they began hurling conjectures: now magic, now poetry, now madness. And he recalls the bitterness: before that they called him Muḥammad the Trustworthy; they were to us a peace, and they became to us a war.

mots.racine: ل ه و / ق ل ب
س ر ر / ن ج و
ب ش ر / م ث ل

versets.ar: قَالَ رَبِّي يَعْلَمُ الْقَوْلَ فِي السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ
versets.tr: qāla rabbī yaʿlamu l-qawla fī s-samāʾi wa-l-arḍi wa-huwa s-samīʿu l-ʿalīmu
versets.trad: He said: “My Lord knows every word in heaven and on earth. He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing.”
versets.commentaire: The words the Real hears are diverse, says Qushayrī: those of creatures among themselves, and those of creatures with the Real; and among those who address Him: the petitioner who asks for this world, the suppliant who desires the delights of the next, and the praiser who glorifies God seeking nothing of this world or the next. Subtler still: He hears the moan of sinners, hidden from creatures lest they be shamed; He hears the intimate prayer of worshippers in the night; and He hears the lament of lovers when ardor seizes them and they cry out from the intensity of longing.

mots.racine: ع ل م / ق و ل
س م ع / ع ل م

versets.ar: بَلْ قَالُوا أَضْغَاثُ أَحْلَامٍ بَلِ افْتَرَاهُ بَلْ هُوَ شَاعِرٌ فَلْيَأْتِنَا بِآيَةٍ كَمَا أُرْسِلَ الْأَوَّلُونَ
versets.tr: bal qālū aḍghāthu aḥlāmin bali ftarāhu bal huwa shāʿirun fal-yaʾtinā bi-āyatin kamā ursila l-awwalūna
versets.trad: But they say: “A jumble of dreams! Rather, he has invented it; rather, he is a poet! Let him bring us a sign, as the ancients were sent.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they kept varying the accusations they leveled at him, after We had sent down to him the command — they who had never observed in him anything but truthfulness in state and in word. The verse paints the instability of denial: dreams, forgery, poetry — the denier endlessly changes his charge, a sign that none holds. She struck me with her own ailment and slipped away, runs the proverb he cites: they project onto him their own confusion.

mots.racine: ض غ ث / ح ل م
ف ر ي
أ ت ي / أ ي ي
mots.sens: let him bring us a sign

versets.ar: مَا آمَنَتْ قَبْلَهُم مِّن قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْنَاهَا ۖ أَفَهُمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ
versets.tr: mā āmanat qablahum min qaryatin ahlaknāhā a-fa-hum yuʾminūna
versets.trad: Before them no town that We destroyed ever believed. Will these, then, believe?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: God has set it as His law to punish those of whom He knows that they will not believe, neither in the present nor in the end. Those who disbelieved in the Messenger's time resemble them in ingratitude, and the Real has decreed for them deprivation and abandonment. Denial is no accident: it reveals a heart already sealed. The town that never believed prefigures the present denier.

mots.racine: أ م ن
ق ر ي / ه ل ك

versets.ar: وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ إِلَّا رِجَالًا نُّوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ ۖ فَاسْأَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ
versets.tr: wa-mā arsalnā qablaka illā rijālan nūḥī ilayhim fa-sʾalū ahla dh-dhikri in kuntum lā taʿlamūna
versets.trad: Before you We sent none but men to whom We sent revelation. So ask the People of the Reminder, if you do not know.
versets.commentaire: When they demanded angels, God recalled that He had only ever sent men, and that their privilege lay in the divine sending itself. Qushayrī interprets “the People of the Reminder”: they are the scholars, the great ones of this community; but more deeply, the people of understanding given by God, those of inspiration, stationed at the place of being informed by the Real. And he sketches the conditions of a fatwa: the jurist judges by lawful effort, but the gnostic (ʿārif) must speak from his inner finding (wajd); otherwise his word is neither received nor heeded.

mots.racine: ر ج ل / و ح ي
س أ ل / ذ ك ر
mots.sens: ask the People of the Reminder
mots.def: ahl al-dhikr: holders of revealed knowledge.

versets.ar: وَمَا جَعَلْنَاهُمْ جَسَدًا لَّا يَأْكُلُونَ الطَّعَامَ وَمَا كَانُوا خَالِدِينَ
versets.tr: wa-mā jaʿalnāhum jasadan lā yaʾkulūna ṭ-ṭaʿāma wa-mā kānū khālidīna
versets.trad: We did not make them bodies that ate no food, nor were they immortal.
versets.commentaire: As they reproached the Prophet for eating, Qushayrī replies: eating does no harm to the inner meaning proper to the great; there is no contradiction between taking food and what hearts and inmost secrets conceal of knowledge. And he tiers the inner degrees: souls know nothing of what hearts live by; the heart knows nothing of what the spirit realizes, and of what is above the spirit, subtler than it: the secret (sirr). As for immortality denied: they are upon a passage; there is no way today, for any created thing, to perpetuity.

mots.racine: ج س د / أ ك ل
خ ل د

versets.ar: ثُمَّ صَدَقْنَاهُمُ الْوَعْدَ فَأَنجَيْنَاهُمْ وَمَن نَّشَاءُ وَأَهْلَكْنَا الْمُسْرِفِينَ
versets.tr: thumma ṣadaqnāhumu l-waʿda fa-anjaynāhum wa-man nashāʾu wa-ahlaknā l-musrifīna
versets.trad: Then We made good Our promise to them: We saved them and whom We willed, and We destroyed the transgressors.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the Real fulfills His promise, even if the time of its fulfillment is delayed. And he specifies the nature of the promised help to the people of Truth: the raising of the word of religion, the humbling of whoever defies the Truth among the deniers, by proof and argument, by clarifying the way of guidance and exposing the error of the people of doubt. The salvation of the messengers and the ruin of the transgressors accomplish a single law: God's fidelity to His word.

mots.racine: ص د ق / و ع د
mots.def: ṣadaqa al-waʿd: to fulfill what was promised.

mots.racine: ه ل ك / س ر ف
mots.def: musrif: one who exceeds all measure.

versets.ar: لَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَا إِلَيْكُمْ كِتَابًا فِيهِ ذِكْرُكُمْ ۖ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ
versets.tr: laqad anzalnā ilaykum kitāban fīhi dhikrukum a-fa-lā taʿqilūna
versets.trad: We have sent down to you a Book in which is your reminder. Will you not reason?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: by the Book He means the Qurʾān; “in which is your reminder” means: your honor and your rank. Whoever takes light from the light it contains is blessed in his here and his hereafter. The Reminder is not only warning: it is the elevation of the one who receives it, a light that ennobles the heart. Not to reason is to deprive oneself of one's own dignity.

mots.racine: ك ت ب / ذ ك ر
mots.sens: a Book in which is your reminder

mots.racine: ع ق ل

versets.ar: وَكَمْ قَصَمْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍ كَانَتْ ظَالِمَةً وَأَنشَأْنَا بَعْدَهَا قَوْمًا آخَرِينَ
versets.tr: wa-kam qaṣamnā min qaryatin kānat ẓālimatan wa-anshaʾnā baʿdahā qawman ākharīna
versets.trad: How many a town that was wrongdoing have We shattered, raising up another people after it.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī internalizes the verse: God grants the wrongdoer respite, then seizes him with a grip of domination and vengeance. Then he transposes: when the servant wrongs his soul, God forbids that grace dwell in it and makes it the abode of abandonment; when he wrongs his heart through heedlessness, He looses upon it the base thoughts that are the devil's whisperings; and likewise when the spirit is ruined: the realities and loves leave it, attachments and familiarities take it over. The shattered town is the image of the heart given over to its ruin.

mots.racine: ق ص م / ق ر ي
ن ش أ / ق و م
mots.sens: We raised another people after it

versets.ar: فَلَمَّا أَحَسُّوا بَأْسَنَا إِذَا هُم مِّنْهَا يَرْكُضُونَ
versets.tr: fa-lammā aḥassū baʾsanā idhā hum minhā yarkuḍūna
versets.trad: When they sensed Our might, at once they fled from it.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: when they tasted the dire consequence of their deeds, they were thrown into turmoil in their states; but their regret availed them nothing, and their feet could not return to their place. And he seals it with a maxim: once treachery has appeared, the trust is no longer accepted. Flight at the hour of punishment is the confession of helplessness: one runs only toward the void.

mots.racine: ح س س / ب أ س
ر ك ض

versets.ar: لَا تَرْكُضُوا وَارْجِعُوا إِلَىٰ مَا أُتْرِفْتُمْ فِيهِ وَمَسَاكِنِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْأَلُونَ
versets.tr: lā tarkuḍū wa-rjiʿū ilā mā utriftum fīhi wa-masākinikum laʿallakum tusʾalūna
versets.trad: “Do not flee! Return to the luxury you enjoyed and to your dwellings — perhaps you will be questioned.”
versets.commentaire: The command — “return to your luxury” — is bitter mockery for those who have lost. Qushayrī: treachery has a spreading; once it occurs, it does not stop. And the image of the wreck: when the ship sinks, nothing remains to the sailor but to show his grief — and how useless that is! They are sent back to dwellings that will shelter them no more: yesterday's comfort becomes today's reckoning.

mots.racine: ر ك ض
ت ر ف
mots.def: utrifa: to be spoiled by abundance.

versets.ar: قَالُوا يَا وَيْلَنَا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَالِمِينَ
versets.tr: qālū yā waylanā innā kunnā ẓālimīna
versets.trad: They said: “Woe to us! We were indeed wrongdoers.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: confession has its time; when its hour has passed, it is as the proverb says: the miser outruns the eager. And he adds: laying down the bow after loosing the arrow is worth nothing. To acknowledge one's wrong at the moment of punishment is to confess when confession no longer redeems. The “woe to us” is true, but it comes out of its time.

mots.racine: و ي ل
ظ ل م

versets.ar: فَمَا زَالَت تِّلْكَ دَعْوَاهُمْ حَتَّىٰ جَعَلْنَاهُمْ حَصِيدًا خَامِدِينَ
versets.tr: fa-mā zālat tilka daʿwāhum ḥattā jaʿalnāhum ḥaṣīdan khāmidīna
versets.trad: And that cry of theirs did not cease until We made them a mown harvest, extinguished.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī unfolds the supreme distress: it is an affliction that a man should complain and not be heard, weep and it avail nothing, draw near and be repelled, fall ill and not be visited, excuse himself and not be forgiven — and the utmost of affliction is annihilation. “A mown harvest, extinguished”: their very clamor becomes the rattle of a fire going out.

mots.racine: د ع و
ح ص د / خ م د

versets.ar: وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَاعِبِينَ
versets.tr: wa-mā khalaqnā s-samāʾa wa-l-arḍa wa-mā baynahumā lāʿibīna
versets.trad: We did not create heaven and earth and what is between them in play.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: play is the attribute of one who has strayed from rectitude, who by his act seeks pleasure and is dragged into the rope of folly — and the right of the Real is too holy for that. Creation is no diversion: it is sign, trial, and call. To see the world as play is to project one's own frivolity onto it; to see it as serious work is to read in it the wisdom of the Maker.

mots.racine: خ ل ق
mots.sens: We did not create heaven and earth

mots.racine: ل ع ب

versets.ar: لَوْ أَرَدْنَا أَن نَّتَّخِذَ لَهْوًا لَّاتَّخَذْنَاهُ مِن لَّدُنَّا إِن كُنَّا فَاعِلِينَ
versets.tr: law aradnā an nattakhidha lahwan lā-ttakhadhnāhu min ladunnā in kunnā fāʿilīna
versets.trad: Had We wished to take up a diversion, We would have taken it from Ourself — if We were ever to do so.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He speaks to them according to their understanding; otherwise, He whom no forgetting touches, no diversion carries off, and the Real admits neither flaw nor equal. The verse is hypothetical so as to refute: God has no need of play, being without lack. Diversion presupposes a void to fill; Fullness knows none.

mots.racine: أ خ ذ / ل ه و
ل د ن

versets.ar: بَلْ نَقْذِفُ بِالْحَقِّ عَلَى الْبَاطِلِ فَيَدْمَغُهُ فَإِذَا هُوَ زَاهِقٌ ۚ وَلَكُمُ الْوَيْلُ مِمَّا تَصِفُونَ
versets.tr: bal naqdhifu bi-l-ḥaqqi ʿalā l-bāṭili fa-yadmaghuhu fa-idhā huwa zāhiqun wa-lakumu l-waylu mimmā taṣifūna
versets.trad: Rather, We hurl the Truth against falsehood; it crushes it, and at once it vanishes. Woe to you for what you ascribe!
versets.commentaire: A magnificent image of Qushayrī: We bring the day of realization over the nights of illusions: the cloud of absence is dispelled, the mist of fancies clears, the sun of certainty shines forth, and the sky of realities is cleansed of all the dust of suspicions. Truth, hurled against falsehood, does not argue: it strikes like lightning. Light does not combat shadow, it abolishes it by appearing.

mots.racine: ق ذ ف / ح ق ق / ب ط ل
ز ه ق

versets.ar: وَلَهُ مَن فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۚ وَمَنْ عِندَهُ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِ وَلَا يَسْتَحْسِرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lahu man fī s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi wa-man ʿindahu lā yastakbirūna ʿan ʿibādatihi wa-lā yastaḥsirūna
versets.trad: To Him belong all who are in the heavens and the earth; and those who are with Him are not too proud to worship Him, nor do they weary.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: events belong to Him in ownership, beings belong to Him in decree; God is too exalted to be adorned by an assent or diminished by an opposition. By the decree all that is appears, and according to the divine choice the word runs its course. Those who are “with Him” — the nearest — worship without pride or fatigue: nearness does not exempt from service, it lightens and perpetuates it.

mots.racine: س م و / أ ر ض
mots.sens: to Him those of the heavens and earth

mots.racine: ك ب ر / ع ب د
mots.sens: they do not disdain His worship

versets.ar: يُسَبِّحُونَ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهَارَ لَا يَفْتُرُونَ
versets.tr: yusabbiḥūna l-layla wa-n-nahāra lā yafturūna
versets.trad: They glorify Him night and day without flagging.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī distinguishes two glorifications: the obedient one endowed with choice glorifies Him by truthful speech; and all creatures glorify Him by the very indication of their createdness, by the proof of their evidence. The tasbīḥ of the angels is voluntary; that of things is mute but real — the existent, by the mere fact of being, attests its Author. To glorify unceasingly is to be pure gaze turned toward Him.

mots.racine: س ب ح
ف ت ر

versets.ar: أَمِ اتَّخَذُوا آلِهَةً مِّنَ الْأَرْضِ هُمْ يُنشِرُونَ
versets.tr: ami ttakhadhū ālihatan mina l-arḍi hum yunshirūna
versets.trad: Or have they taken gods from the earth who can raise the dead?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the Real is alone in creating and bringing into being; He is too holy to have likes and equals. Those worshipped besides Him are dead, not living — and this is known of necessity. The argument is simple: what cannot give life cannot be a god. To worship what does not raise the dead is to entrust one's destiny to a nothingness.

mots.racine: أ خ ذ / أ ل ه
ن ش ر
mots.def: anshara: to bring back to life.

versets.ar: لَوْ كَانَ فِيهِمَا آلِهَةٌ إِلَّا اللَّهُ لَفَسَدَتَا ۚ فَسُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ رَبِّ الْعَرْشِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ
versets.tr: law kāna fīhimā ālihatun illā llāhu la-fasadatā fa-subḥāna llāhi rabbi l-ʿarshi ʿammā yaṣifūna
versets.trad: Were there in them gods besides God, both would have fallen to ruin. Glory be to God, Lord of the Throne, above what they ascribe!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī draws the argument from the unity of governance: any affair entrusted to several does not run in order, for dispute and disagreement arise among them. Then he contemplates the harmony: the heaven in its height turns its spheres without a pillar to hold them; the earth is fixed in its regions by the ordered alternation of its night and day; sun, moon, and wandering stars turn within the constellations, and the celestial vault spreads without the least fissure. The order of the world is the signature of the One.

mots.racine: أ ل ه
ف س د
mots.def: fasada: to spoil, fall into disorder.

mots.racine: ع ر ش

versets.ar: لَا يُسْأَلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ وَهُمْ يُسْأَلُونَ
versets.tr: lā yusʾalu ʿammā yafʿalu wa-hum yusʾalūna
versets.trad: He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned.
versets.commentaire: A luminous concision from Qushayrī: because creation belongs to Him, He is not questioned; and they are questioned because His right is binding upon them. The absolute Master gives no account, for none is above Him to judge Him. The servant is accountable, for he lives under a right that precedes him. God's freedom and man's responsibility are of a single fabric.

mots.racine: س أ ل / ف ع ل
mots.sens: He is not questioned about what He does

mots.racine: س أ ل

versets.ar: أَمِ اتَّخَذُوا مِن دُونِهِ آلِهَةً ۖ قُلْ هَاتُوا بُرْهَانَكُمْ ۖ هَٰذَا ذِكْرُ مَن مَّعِيَ وَذِكْرُ مَن قَبْلِي ۗ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ الْحَقَّ ۖ فَهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ
versets.tr: ami ttakhadhū min dūnihi ālihatan qul hātū burhānakum hādhā dhikru man maʿiya wa-dhikru man qablī bal aktharuhum lā yaʿlamūna l-ḥaqqa fa-hum muʿriḍūna
versets.trad: Or have they taken gods besides Him? Say: “Bring your proof! This is the reminder of those with me and the reminder of those before me.” But most of them do not know the Truth, so they turn away.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī draws several lessons: it shows the falsity of blind imitation and the obligation to establish argument and proof; it affirms the oneness of the Worshipped; and it establishes the acquisition (kasb) of the servants, for without it blame and reproach would not be addressed to them. And he warns: whoever attaches his heart to a created thing, or imagines he receives anything from other than God, enters into the crowd of these people. “This is the reminder of those with me and those before”: religion is one — the attestation of the One.

mots.racine: ب ر ه ن

mots.ar: ذِكْرُ مَن مَّعِيَ وَذِكْرُ مَن قَبْلِي
mots.tr: dhikru man maʿiya wa-dhikru man qablī
mots.racine: ذ ك ر
mots.sens: the reminder of those with me and before me

mots.racine: ع ل م / ح ق ق
mots.sens: they do not know the Truth

versets.ar: وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا نُوحِي إِلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاعْبُدُونِ
versets.tr: wa-mā arsalnā min qablika min rasūlin illā nūḥī ilayhi annahu lā ilāha illā anā fa-ʿbudūni
versets.trad: We sent no messenger before you but We revealed to him that there is no god but I; so worship Me.
themes: oneness, the heart of all prophecy
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: tawḥīd is one in every revealed Law, and worship is a duty for whoever receives a messenger; but the acts are exposed to abrogation and change, whereas tawḥīd and the way that leads to it admit neither abrogation nor substitution. The rites vary from prophet to prophet; the attestation of the One, never. It is the single thread running through the whole chain of messengers.

mots.racine: أ ل ه
ع ب د

versets.ar: وَقَالُوا اتَّخَذَ الرَّحْمَٰنُ وَلَدًا ۗ سُبْحَانَهُ ۚ بَلْ عِبَادٌ مُّكْرَمُونَ
versets.tr: wa-qālū ttakhadha r-raḥmānu waladan subḥānahu bal ʿibādun mukramūna
versets.trad: They said: “The All-Merciful has taken offspring!” Glory be to Him! Rather, they are honored servants.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī notes a rule of method: there is in this verse a permission to cite the sayings of the people of error and innovation, but in the mode of refutation, of exposing their flaws and warning of their mistakes. Thus reporting error is licit only to confound it. The angels and the prophets are no “offspring” of God: they are “honored servants” — the supreme honor is to be a servant, not a son.

mots.racine: و ل د
ع ب د / ك ر م

versets.ar: لَا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُم بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ
versets.tr: lā yasbiqūnahu bi-l-qawli wa-hum bi-amrihi yaʿmalūna
versets.trad: They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He informs that the angels are preserved from any opposition to His command, and that they fall short in no duty incumbent upon them. Not to precede His word is the perfect effacement of one's own will: the angel anticipates nothing, adds nothing, takes away nothing — pure instrument of the divine command. A model of the servant whose will has melted into his Master's.

mots.racine: س ب ق / ق و ل
mots.sens: they do not precede Him in speech

mots.racine: أ م ر / ع م ل

versets.ar: يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ وَهُم مِّنْ خَشْيَتِهِ مُشْفِقُونَ
versets.tr: yaʿlamu mā bayna aydīhim wa-mā khalfahum wa-lā yashfaʿūna illā li-mani rtaḍā wa-hum min khashyatihi mushfiqūna
versets.trad: He knows what is before them and what is behind them; they intercede only for those whom He approves, and they tremble in awe of Him.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: His eternal knowledge does not attach to one object to the exclusion of another; it embraces all knowables, and nothing escapes Him. On intercession: “they intercede only for whom He approves” proves that they intercede for some, and that God accepts their intercession. And on their awe, finely: they have no sin, yet they tremble — proof that He could punish them; for if it were impossible to punish the innocent, they would not fear Him, knowing they have committed no fault.

mots.racine: ي د ي / خ ل ف
mots.sens: what is before and behind them

mots.racine: ش ف ع / ر ض و
mots.sens: they intercede only for the approved
mots.def: shafaʿa: to intercede; irtaḍā: to approve.

mots.racine: خ ش ي / ش ف ق

versets.ar: وَمَن يَقُلْ مِنْهُمْ إِنِّي إِلَٰهٌ مِّن دُونِهِ فَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِيهِ جَهَنَّمَ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِي الظَّالِمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-man yaqul minhum innī ilāhun min dūnihi fa-dhālika najzīhi jahannama kadhālika najzī ẓ-ẓālimīna
versets.trad: And whoever of them should say, “I am a god besides Him,” him We would recompense with Hell. Thus do We recompense the wrongdoers.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī notes the subtlety: He has already said that they are preserved from any fault; and yet He knows that they will never say this — but He knows how the judgment would be if it happened. And he concludes, dizzyingly: the Real knows how that which will not be would be. Divine knowledge reaches even the unrealized possible: God knows the judgment of what will never come to pass.

mots.racine: أ ل ه
mots.sens: I am a god besides Him

mots.racine: ج ز ي / ج ه ن م

versets.ar: أَوَلَمْ يَرَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ كَانَتَا رَتْقًا فَفَتَقْنَاهُمَا ۖ وَجَعَلْنَا مِنَ الْمَاءِ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ حَيٍّ ۖ أَفَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ
versets.tr: a-wa-lam yara lladhīna kafarū anna s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍa kānatā ratqan fa-fataqnāhumā wa-jaʿalnā mina l-māʾi kulla shayʾin ḥayyin a-fa-lā yuʾminūna
versets.trad: Do the disbelievers not see that the heavens and the earth were a closed mass, and We split them apart, and We made from water every living thing? Will they not believe?
versets.commentaire: Before their doubt about the new creation, God sets up the proof of the first. Then Qushayrī rises from the visible waters to the hidden: the life of souls is by the water of heaven, on the side of nourishment; the life of hearts by the water of mercy; the life of secrets by the water of veneration; and there are those whose life is by the water of modesty (ḥayāʾ) — and they are rare. From the single water proceed all lives, from the body to the subtlest secret.

mots.racine: ر ت ق / ف ت ق
mots.sens: they were fused, We split them
mots.def: ratq: closed mass; fataqa: to part.

mots.racine: م و ه / ح ي ي

versets.ar: وَجَعَلْنَا فِي الْأَرْضِ رَوَاسِيَ أَن تَمِيدَ بِهِمْ وَجَعَلْنَا فِيهَا فِجَاجًا سُبُلًا لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ
versets.tr: wa-jaʿalnā fī l-arḍi rawāsiya an tamīda bihim wa-jaʿalnā fīhā fijājan subulan laʿallahum yahtadūna
versets.trad: We set firm mountains on the earth lest it sway with them, and made therein broad passes as ways, that they might be guided.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī internalizes the mountains: the saints (awliyāʾ) are the firm anchors of the earth; through them people are provided for, through them affliction is averted, through them gifts are poured down. As without the mountains the earth would have no anchorage, so without the masters who are its pegs, calamity would descend. And the ways: as the earth has roads to reach its goals, so the ways to Him are traced by the guidance the masters open to disciples on the road to God.

mots.racine: ر س و / م ي د
ف ج ج / س ب ل

versets.ar: وَجَعَلْنَا السَّمَاءَ سَقْفًا مَّحْفُوظًا ۖ وَهُمْ عَنْ آيَاتِهَا مُعْرِضُونَ
versets.tr: wa-jaʿalnā s-samāʾa saqfan maḥfūẓan wa-hum ʿan āyātihā muʿriḍūna
versets.trad: We made the sky a protected canopy, yet they turn away from its signs.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sets up a correspondence between the two worlds: in the outward, the sky is luminous and the earth inhabited; likewise souls have lands that are the dwellings of acts of obedience, and in the sky of hearts there are the stars of intelligence, the moons of knowledge, the suns of tawḥīd and gnosis. And as the stars repel devils, the knowledges repel devils; but the common folk turn away from the signs of hearts, from their lights, not knowing them — only the elect discern them.

mots.racine: س ق ف / ح ف ظ
أ ي ي / ع ر ض

versets.ar: وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهَارَ وَالشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ ۖ كُلٌّ فِي فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ
versets.tr: wa-huwa lladhī khalaqa l-layla wa-n-nahāra wa-sh-shamsa wa-l-qamara kullun fī falakin yasbaḥūna
versets.trad: It is He who created night and day, the sun and the moon, each gliding in an orbit.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reads in the alternation of the heavenly bodies the spiritual states: as the Real wraps night over day, so He brings into the day of expansion (basṭ) the night of contraction (qabḍ). And the distinction of men: the sun is always in its sign, neither waxing nor waning — thus the master of established tawḥīd (tamkīn) rises from examining the proof to the spirit of evidence; the moon now wanes, now shines — thus the master of knowledge, sometimes brought back to renewing his gaze, sometimes veiled: he is the man of variation (talwīn).

mots.racine: خ ل ق
ف ل ك / س ب ح
mots.def: falak: orbit; sabaḥa: to swim, to glide.

versets.ar: وَمَا جَعَلْنَا لِبَشَرٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ الْخُلْدَ ۖ أَفَإِن مِّتَّ فَهُمُ الْخَالِدُونَ
versets.tr: wa-mā jaʿalnā li-basharin min qablika l-khulda a-fa-in mitta fa-humu l-khālidūna
versets.trad: We granted immortality to no mortal before you. If you die, will they then live forever?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī softens the verse with an intimacy: you are in this world a wayfarer on the road; but We have not left you alone in the world — which is why the Prophet said to his companion in the cave: what do you think of two whose third is God? The Prophet's mortality is no abandonment: the wayfarer is never alone, for the divine companionship does not leave him even at the threshold of death.

mots.racine: ب ش ر / خ ل د
mots.sens: to a mortal before you immortality

mots.racine: م و ت / خ ل د
mots.sens: if you die, will they live forever?

versets.ar: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذَائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ ۗ وَنَبْلُوكُم بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً ۖ وَإِلَيْنَا تُرْجَعُونَ
versets.tr: kullu nafsin dhāʾiqatu l-mawti wa-nablūkum bi-sh-sharri wa-l-khayri fitnatan wa-ilaynā turjaʿūna
versets.trad: Every soul shall taste death. We test you with evil and with good as a trial, and to Us you shall be returned.
themes: the trial of good and evil
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī meditates on the diversity of deaths: death is a scourge for some, a rest for others; for some, the end of the time of longing; for others, the opening of the door of separation; for some, the moment when affliction descends; for others, deliverance from their torment; for some trial and great upheaval, for others healing and safety. One death, and as many faces as there are hearts. The trial by good and evil tests alike: the gift can seduce as the misfortune can break.

mots.racine: ن ف س / ذ و ق / م و ت
ب ل و / ش ر ر / خ ي ر / ف ت ن
mots.sens: We test you with evil and good

versets.ar: وَإِذَا رَآكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِن يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُوًا أَهَٰذَا الَّذِي يَذْكُرُ آلِهَتَكُمْ وَهُم بِذِكْرِ الرَّحْمَٰنِ هُمْ كَافِرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-idhā raʾāka lladhīna kafarū in yattakhidhūnaka illā huzuwan a-hādhā lladhī yadhkuru ālihatakum wa-hum bi-dhikri r-raḥmāni hum kāfirūna
versets.trad: When the disbelievers see you, they only take you in mockery: “Is this the one who speaks of your gods?” — while they disbelieve in the mention of the All-Merciful.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: had they seen in him the attributes of election and the rank to which He raised him, they would have prostrated before him; but they were veiled from his inner senses and his secret, and beheld only his body and his form. Mockery is born of blindness: they see only the shell and ridicule the hidden treasure. The veil is not upon the Prophet, but upon their eyes.

mots.racine: أ خ ذ / ه ز أ
mots.sens: they take you only in mockery

mots.racine: ذ ك ر / ك ف ر
mots.sens: in the All-Merciful's mention they disbelieve

versets.ar: خُلِقَ الْإِنسَانُ مِنْ عَجَلٍ ۚ سَأُرِيكُمْ آيَاتِي فَلَا تَسْتَعْجِلُونِ
versets.tr: khuliqa l-insānu min ʿajalin sa-urīkum āyātī fa-lā tastaʿjilūni
versets.trad: Man was created of haste. I will show you My signs, so do not ask Me to hurry.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī distinguishes two eagernesses: haste is blameworthy, promptness is praiseworthy. Promptness (musāraʿa) is to set out toward a thing at the beginning of its time; haste (ʿajala) is to anticipate it before its time. Haste is the fruit of the devil's whispering, promptness is the affair of grace. Man wants everything, and at once: to be hasty is to want before the hour what wisdom ripens in its time.

mots.racine: خ ل ق / ع ج ل
ع ج ل

versets.ar: وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَٰذَا الْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ
versets.tr: wa-yaqūlūna matā hādhā l-waʿdu in kuntum ṣādiqīna
versets.trad: They say: “When will this promise be, if you are truthful?”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: accustomed to denying the prophets in what they promised them, they demanded in haste what they were threatened with; but had they known what awaited them, they would have kept silent — their very eagerness betrays their dread. To demand the punishment in defiance is to be ignorant of what one calls for. The haste to require the promise is the unwitting confession that one does not believe it.

mots.racine: و ع د
ص د ق

versets.ar: لَوْ يَعْلَمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا حِينَ لَا يَكُفُّونَ عَن وُجُوهِهِمُ النَّارَ وَلَا عَن ظُهُورِهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ
versets.tr: law yaʿlamu lladhīna kafarū ḥīna lā yakuffūna ʿan wujūhihimu n-nāra wa-lā ʿan ẓuhūrihim wa-lā hum yunṣarūna
versets.trad: If only the disbelievers knew the moment when they will not hold the Fire off their faces or their backs, and will not be helped...
themes: the ignorance of the appointed hour
versets.commentaire: The sentence hangs suspended, and Qushayrī completes it: ...if they knew, they would today guard against plunging into the torment of conjectures and the delusion of the devil's promises. Their ignorance of the hour of the Fire is precisely what emboldens them; knowledge would freeze them with terror. The veil over the future is what makes their present heedlessness possible.

mots.racine: ك ف ف / و ج ه / ن و ر
mots.sens: they do not hold the Fire off their faces

mots.racine: ن ص ر

versets.ar: بَلْ تَأْتِيهِم بَغْتَةً فَتَبْهَتُهُمْ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ رَدَّهَا وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ
versets.tr: bal taʾtīhim baghtatan fa-tabhatuhum fa-lā yastaṭīʿūna raddahā wa-lā hum yunẓarūna
versets.trad: Rather, it will come upon them suddenly and stun them; they will not be able to repel it, nor will they be granted respite.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: punishment, when it comes unexpectedly, is more searing and more severe; and God's way in retribution is to raise the wind of suddenness at the moment one is immersed in blessing and grace. The stunning (baht) strikes all the harder because one thought oneself safe. The respite refused seals the instant: no delay for one who would not have the warning.

mots.racine: أ ت ي / ب غ ت / ب ه ت
mots.sens: it comes suddenly and stuns them
mots.def: baghta: unexpectedly; bahata: to strike dumb.

mots.racine: ط و ع / ر د د

versets.ar: وَلَقَدِ اسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَحَاقَ بِالَّذِينَ سَخِرُوا مِنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِئُونَ
versets.tr: wa-laqadi stuhziʾa bi-rusulin min qablika fa-ḥāqa bi-lladhīna sakhirū minhum mā kānū bihi yastahziʾūna
versets.trad: Messengers before you were mocked, but what they mocked encompassed the mockers.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: it is a consolation for him, and the announcement of swift triumph over those who persecuted him among his enemies: soon you will see fall upon them the weight of the punishment they have earned. Mockery is a boomerang: what one laughs at returns to strike the laugher. The story of the ancient prophets consoles the present and threatens the mocker.

mots.racine: ه ز أ / ر س ل
ح ي ق / س خ ر
mots.def: ḥāqa: to envelop, to recoil upon.

versets.ar: قُلْ مَن يَكْلَؤُكُم بِاللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهَارِ مِنَ الرَّحْمَٰنِ ۗ بَلْ هُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ رَبِّهِم مُّعْرِضُونَ
versets.tr: qul man yaklaʾukum bi-l-layli wa-n-nahāri mina r-raḥmāni bal hum ʿan dhikri rabbihim muʿriḍūna
versets.trad: Say: “Who guards you, night and day, from the All-Merciful?” Yet they turn away from the remembrance of their Lord.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: it establishes for them that their salvation does not come from the intervention of creatures — they have tried this in their distresses. How do they not disavow what is nothing to them, what has neither profit nor harm? And he draws from it for the believers: their needs for good, profit and defense, come from God alone; the duty, then, is the constant retreat of their hearts at the threshold of His generosity.

mots.racine: ك ل أ
mots.sens: who guards you night and day?

mots.racine: ذ ك ر / ع ر ض
mots.sens: from their Lord's remembrance turning away

versets.ar: أَمْ لَهُمْ آلِهَةٌ تَمْنَعُهُم مِّن دُونِنَا ۚ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَلَا هُم مِّنَّا يُصْحَبُونَ
versets.tr: am lahum ālihatun tamnaʿuhum min dūninā lā yastaṭīʿūna naṣra anfusihim wa-lā hum minnā yuṣḥabūna
versets.trad: Or have they gods besides Us who can defend them? They cannot help themselves, nor can they be protected from Us.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He extends and repeats the discourse to make them recognize the impossibility that profit or harm should come from inert objects and the idols they worshipped; and from all this repetition of words, nothing is drawn from them but impotence and forced silence. The idols cannot even help themselves: how then would they help others? The hammering of the argument leaves the adversary nothing but muteness.

mots.racine: أ ل ه / م ن ع
mots.sens: gods who protect them against Us

mots.racine: ن ص ر / ن ف س

versets.ar: بَلْ مَتَّعْنَا هَٰؤُلَاءِ وَآبَاءَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ طَالَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْعُمُرُ ۗ أَفَلَا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّا نَأْتِي الْأَرْضَ نَنقُصُهَا مِنْ أَطْرَافِهَا ۚ أَفَهُمُ الْغَالِبُونَ
versets.tr: bal mattaʿnā hāʾulāʾi wa-ābāʾahum ḥattā ṭāla ʿalayhimu l-ʿumuru a-fa-lā yarawna annā naʾtī l-arḍa nanquṣuhā min aṭrāfihā a-fa-humu l-ghālibūna
versets.trad: Rather, We let these and their fathers enjoy until life grew long for them. Do they not see that We come to the land, reducing it from its borders? Are they then the victors?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: a long enjoyment, if not joined to grace and accompanied by preservation, is but a ruse and a gradual leading-on (istidrāj), an increase of punishment; for the Real, as He punishes by pains, punishes by respite and delay. And the “reduction of the land”: it shrinks by the passing of the great, and the base ones remain. Then, wistful, these verses on decline: every day I see myself diminish, and with the lessening nothing remains.

mots.racine: م ت ع / أ ب و
mots.sens: We let these and their fathers enjoy

mots.racine: أ ت ي / ن ق ص / ط ر ف
mots.sens: We reduce the land from its borders

versets.ar: قُلْ إِنَّمَا أُنذِرُكُم بِالْوَحْيِ ۚ وَلَا يَسْمَعُ الصُّمُّ الدُّعَاءَ إِذَا مَا يُنذَرُونَ
versets.tr: qul innamā undhirukum bi-l-waḥyi wa-lā yasmaʿu ṣ-ṣummu d-duʿāʾa idhā mā yundharūna
versets.trad: Say: “I only warn you by revelation”; but the deaf do not hear the call when they are warned.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: that is: by God's command I make known to you the place of fear; it is revealed to me concerning you to frighten you of His painful punishment; but one deprived of the hearing of grace — what use to him is the repetition of the call to accept? Deafness is not of the ear but of the heart: one can hear the words and remain deaf to the meaning. The warning awakens only one who already has the inner ear.

mots.racine: ن ذ ر / و ح ي
mots.sens: I warn you only by revelation

mots.racine: س م ع / ص م م / د ع و
mots.sens: the deaf do not hear the call

versets.ar: وَلَئِن مَّسَّتْهُمْ نَفْحَةٌ مِّنْ عَذَابِ رَبِّكَ لَيَقُولُنَّ يَا وَيْلَنَا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَالِمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-laʾin massathum nafḥatun min ʿadhābi rabbika la-yaqūlunna yā waylanā innā kunnā ẓālimīna
versets.trad: Yet if a breath of your Lord's punishment were to touch them, they would surely cry: “Woe to us! We were indeed wrongdoers.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: that is: they cannot bear the least part of the punishment; and the Real, if He wishes to make someone suffer, needs neither reinforcement nor aid. A mere “breath” (nafḥa) of the torment suffices to break their arrogance and wring out the confession. What they denied in bravado, the slightest touch makes them confess: hardness of heart holds only in the absence of trial.

mots.racine: ن ف ح / ع ذ ب
mots.sens: a breath of your Lord's punishment

mots.racine: ظ ل م

versets.ar: وَنَضَعُ الْمَوَازِينَ الْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ فَلَا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْئًا ۖ وَإِن كَانَ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍ مِّنْ خَرْدَلٍ أَتَيْنَا بِهَا ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِنَا حَاسِبِينَ
versets.tr: wa-naḍaʿu l-mawāzīna l-qisṭa li-yawmi l-qiyāmati fa-lā tuẓlamu nafsun shayʾan wa-in kāna mithqāla ḥabbatin min khardalin ataynā bihā wa-kafā binā ḥāsibīna
versets.trad: We shall set up the just balances for the Day of Resurrection, so that no soul is wronged in anything. Even were it the weight of a mustard seed, We would bring it forth. Sufficient are We as reckoners.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī internalizes the balances: deeds are weighed on the balance of sincerity (ikhlāṣ): what has none is not accepted; states are weighed on the balance of truthfulness (ṣidq): what mingles with self-satisfaction is not accepted; breaths are weighed on the balance of purity: what holds portions of ego and familiarity is not accepted. And he adds: what was for other than God is not worth accepting. Down to the mustard seed, nothing escapes — neither the tiny good, nor the secret portion of ego.

mots.racine: و ز ن / ق س ط
ث ق ل / ح ب ب / خ ر د ل
mots.sens: the weight of a mustard seed

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ وَهَارُونَ الْفُرْقَانَ وَضِيَاءً وَذِكْرًا لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ
versets.tr: wa-laqad ātaynā mūsā wa-hārūna l-furqāna wa-ḍiyāʾan wa-dhikran lil-muttaqīna
versets.trad: We gave Moses and Aaron the Criterion, a light and a reminder for the godfearing,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: what the Real gave the prophets — clarity, light, argument and proof — the believers of their communities share by taking light from it; likewise the great ones of this community share with our Prophet the illumination by the light of certainty. And he defines the godfearing one (muttaqī): one who withdraws from what occupies him and veils him from God; he guards against the causes of the veil and their occasions.

mots.racine: ف ر ق / ض و أ
ذ ك ر / و ق ي

versets.ar: الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُم بِالْغَيْبِ وَهُم مِّنَ السَّاعَةِ مُشْفِقُونَ
versets.tr: alladhīna yakhshawna rabbahum bi-l-ghaybi wa-hum mina s-sāʿati mushfiqūna
versets.trad: those who fear their Lord in the unseen and are in dread of the Hour.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: by the merit of these insights they have fear in the unseen: the lowered silence of the inmost self; and at the time of presence, the foreboding apprehension of a lapse of adab, the fear lest there appear from the hidden what would veil the servant. And he distinguishes two dreads of the Hour: the fear of the Hour promised to the common folk, and the fear of the hour that is the resurrection proper to these people — their inner Day is enacted even now.

mots.racine: خ ش ي / غ ي ب
mots.sens: they fear their Lord in the unseen
mots.def: khashiya: to dread; ghayb: the unseen.

mots.racine: س و ع / ش ف ق
mots.sens: of the Hour they are in dread

versets.ar: وَهَٰذَا ذِكْرٌ مُّبَارَكٌ أَنزَلْنَاهُ ۚ أَفَأَنتُمْ لَهُ مُنكِرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-hādhā dhikrun mubārakun anzalnāhu a-fa-antum lahu munkirūna
versets.trad: This is a blessed Reminder that We have sent down. Will you then deny it?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He described the Qurʾān as “blessed” (mubārak), which announces its permanence — from their expression: the bird “made baraka” upon the water, that is, it remained there. And he rises: this Book, no falsehood reaches it, neither before nor behind; and what has no beginning — His eternal Word — has no end for the Book that points to it. The blessing of the Qurʾān is its very duration: it is not exhausted, for it bears the Eternal.

mots.racine: ذ ك ر / ب ر ك
ن ك ر

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ رُشْدَهُ مِن قَبْلُ وَكُنَّا بِهِ عَالِمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-laqad ātaynā ibrāhīma rushdahu min qablu wa-kunnā bihi ʿālimīna
versets.trad: We had granted Abraham his right guidance before, and We knew him well.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He means the guidance He made known to him, such that he did not pronounce for what admits decline and setting — had He not distinguished him from the beginning by this knowledge. How would he have been guided to the distinction between himself and the created, but for the light of tawḥīd with which He illumined him before his very examination of the created? And higher still: it is what He unveiled to his spirit before creating it, by the manifestation of the Reality. Abraham's rectitude was a gift prior to all seeking.

mots.racine: أ ت ي / ر ش د
ع ل م

versets.ar: إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِ مَا هَٰذِهِ التَّمَاثِيلُ الَّتِي أَنتُمْ لَهَا عَاكِفُونَ
versets.tr: idh qāla li-abīhi wa-qawmihi mā hādhihi t-tamāthīlu llatī antum lahā ʿākifūna
versets.trad: When he said to his father and his people: “What are these statues to which you cling in devotion?”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: he addressed his people and his father with awakening admonition, hoping they would rouse from the intoxication of heedlessness, return from the darkness of harshness, and emerge from the straits of doubt. Then he asked God to aid them by seeking their guidance. But when it became clear to him that they would not believe and would persist in their denial, he disavowed them all. Love calls to awakening; but obstinacy compels rupture.

mots.racine: م ث ل
ع ك ف
mots.def: ʿākif: one given to a cult.

versets.ar: قَالُوا وَجَدْنَا آبَاءَنَا لَهَا عَابِدِينَ
versets.tr: qālū wajadnā ābāʾanā lahā ʿābidīna
versets.trad: They said: “We found our fathers worshipping them.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they found nothing to answer but blind imitation (taqlīd). Their only argument is the custom of the fathers — not a proof, but a habit. To follow the forefathers in error is to repeat their straying without examining it. Abraham will break this chain by demanding reason, not custom.

mots.racine: و ج د / أ ب و
ع ب د

versets.ar: قَالَ لَقَدْ كُنتُمْ أَنتُمْ وَآبَاؤُكُمْ فِي ضَلَالٍ مُّبِينٍ
versets.tr: qāla laqad kuntum antum wa-ābāʾukum fī ḍalālin mubīnin
versets.trad: He said: “Indeed you and your fathers have been in manifest error.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: he judged with equity, placing them and their fathers on the same rank in error; the argument that turned against their forefathers turns against them. They did not accept that he should blame their fathers. Abraham refuses the deference owed to tradition when it is false: the fathers' error does not become truth by age. Blind veneration passes on its straying from generation to generation.

mots.racine: أ ب و
mots.sens: you and your fathers have been

mots.racine: ض ل ل / ب ي ن

versets.ar: قَالُوا أَجِئْتَنَا بِالْحَقِّ أَمْ أَنتَ مِنَ اللَّاعِبِينَ
versets.tr: qālū a-jiʾtanā bi-l-ḥaqqi am anta mina l-lāʿibīna
versets.trad: They said: “Have you come to us with the truth, or are you one of those who jest?”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they demanded of him the proof of what he called them to, faith. Unable to refute, they doubt the seriousness of the messenger: “do you speak in earnest, or in play?” It is the evasion of one who, having no argument left, calls the intentions into question. Abraham's answer will be to turn their gaze toward the Creator of the heavens and the earth.

mots.racine: ج ي أ / ح ق ق
mots.sens: have you come with the truth?

mots.racine: ل ع ب
mots.sens: or are you of those who jest?

versets.ar: قَالَ بَل رَّبُّكُمْ رَبُّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ الَّذِي فَطَرَهُنَّ وَأَنَا عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكُم مِّنَ الشَّاهِدِينَ
versets.tr: qāla bal rabbukum rabbu s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi lladhī faṭarahunna wa-anā ʿalā dhālikum mina sh-shāhidīna
versets.trad: He said: “Rather, your Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth, who originated them, and I am one of those who bear witness to that.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: he referred them to examination, inference, and knowledge through the proofs of reason; for the establishing of the Maker is not known through miracles — miracles are proof of the truthfulness of the prophets, and that is a branch of the knowledge of the Maker. Abraham grounds faith in reason contemplating the created: before believing in the messenger, one must know Him who sends him.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / س م و / أ ر ض
mots.sens: Lord of the heavens and earth

mots.racine: ف ط ر

versets.ar: وَتَاللَّهِ لَأَكِيدَنَّ أَصْنَامَكُم بَعْدَ أَن تُوَلُّوا مُدْبِرِينَ
versets.tr: wa-ta-llāhi la-akīdanna aṣnāmakum baʿda an tuwallū mudbirīna
versets.trad: By God, I will surely plot against your idols after you have turned your backs.”
versets.commentaire: Abraham's oath announces the act that will demonstrate the impotence of the idols. Qushayrī shows that Abraham acts in pure trust: he cared nothing for the harm that would reach him, certain that God alone creates, and that none holds harm for him besides God. The plot is no evil cunning but a demonstration: by shattering the statues, he will force his adversaries to admit that they cannot even defend themselves.

mots.racine: ك ي د / ص ن م
mots.sens: I will plot against your idols

mots.racine: و ل ي / د ب ر

versets.ar: فَجَعَلَهُمْ جُذَاذًا إِلَّا كَبِيرًا لَّهُمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِ يَرْجِعُونَ
versets.tr: fa-jaʿalahum judhādhan illā kabīran lahum laʿallahum ilayhi yarjiʿūna
versets.trad: So he broke them to pieces, all but the largest of them, that they might turn back to it.
versets.commentaire: Abraham reduces the idols to fragments (judhādh), sparing the largest so that the proof might break forth from their own mouths. Qushayrī stresses that the whole act aims to confound the worship of the inert: how can one deem it lawful to worship inert matter, incapable of warding off harm from itself? To leave the great idol is to hold up to the idolaters the mirror of their own absurdity — let them ask their god, then, if it can speak.

mots.racine: ج ع ل / ج ذ ذ
ك ب ر

versets.ar: قَالُوا مَن فَعَلَ هَٰذَا بِآلِهَتِنَا إِنَّهُ لَمِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ
versets.tr: qālū man faʿala hādhā bi-ālihatinā innahu la-mina ẓ-ẓālimīna
versets.trad: They said: “Who has done this to our gods? He is surely one of the wrongdoers!”
versets.commentaire: Discovering the wreckage, they cry injustice — without seeing that the injustice is their very worship. Qushayrī shows the reversal: they defend gods who could not defend themselves. To call “wrongdoer” the one who breaks impotent stones is unwittingly to confess the vanity of those stones: a true god would have needed no avengers.

mots.racine: ف ع ل / أ ل ه
mots.sens: who did this to our gods?

mots.racine: ظ ل م

versets.ar: قَالُوا سَمِعْنَا فَتًى يَذْكُرُهُمْ يُقَالُ لَهُ إِبْرَاهِيمُ
versets.tr: qālū samiʿnā fatan yadhkuruhum yuqālu lahu ibrāhīmu
versets.trad: They said: “We heard a youth mentioning them; he is called Abraham.”
versets.commentaire: They point to the “youth” (fatā) who spoke of the idols with scorn. Qushayrī recalls that Abraham took on the trial in trust: certain that none holds harm for him besides God, he did not fear being named. The term “fatā” — the young knight — will become in the Sufi tradition the very title of futuwwa: the spiritual hero who shatters the inner idols.

mots.racine: س م ع / ف ت ي / ذ ك ر
mots.sens: we heard a youth mention them

mots.racine: ق و ل

versets.ar: قَالُوا فَأْتُوا بِهِ عَلَىٰ أَعْيُنِ النَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَشْهَدُونَ
versets.tr: qālū fa-ʾtū bihi ʿalā aʿyuni n-nāsi laʿallahum yashhadūna
versets.trad: They said: “Bring him before the eyes of the people, that they may bear witness.”
versets.commentaire: Abraham is summoned in broad daylight, that the crowd may witness against him. But Qushayrī shows that it is Abraham who will turn the trial into a demonstration: brought to be confounded, he will confound. The court of the idolaters will become the stage where the evidence of tawḥīd bursts forth before all — the accusers' trap recoils.

mots.racine: أ ت ي / ع ي ن / ن و س
mots.sens: bring him before the people's eyes

mots.racine: ش ه د

versets.ar: قَالُوا أَأَنتَ فَعَلْتَ هَٰذَا بِآلِهَتِنَا يَا إِبْرَاهِيمُ
versets.tr: qālū a-anta faʿalta hādhā bi-ālihatinā yā ibrāhīmu
versets.trad: They said: “Did you do this to our gods, O Abraham?”
versets.commentaire: The interrogation begins. The very question betrays their confusion: they question a man about the fate of their “gods,” instead of questioning the gods. Qushayrī makes of the scene a lesson in method: Abraham will refer them to their own incoherence. To ask a human what he did to the idols is already to admit that the idols know nothing.

mots.racine: ف ع ل
أ ل ه

versets.ar: قَالَ بَلْ فَعَلَهُ كَبِيرُهُمْ هَٰذَا فَاسْأَلُوهُمْ إِن كَانُوا يَنطِقُونَ
versets.tr: qāla bal faʿalahu kabīruhum hādhā fa-sʾalūhum in kānū yanṭiqūna
versets.trad: He said: “Rather, the largest of them did it. Ask them, if they can speak!”
versets.commentaire: Abraham's reply is a brilliant logical trap. Qushayrī relates the dialogue: they said: how should we accuse it, how do you refer us to questioning it, an inert thing? He said: and how do you deem it lawful to worship the inert, incapable of warding off harm from itself? By attributing the act to the great idol and inviting them to question it, Abraham forces them to confess for themselves that the idols do not speak — and so are nothing.

mots.racine: ف ع ل / ك ب ر
س أ ل / ن ط ق

versets.ar: فَرَجَعُوا إِلَىٰ أَنفُسِهِمْ فَقَالُوا إِنَّكُمْ أَنتُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ
versets.tr: fa-rajaʿū ilā anfusihim fa-qālū innakum antumu ẓ-ẓālimūna
versets.trad: So they returned to themselves and said: “It is you who are the wrongdoers!”
versets.commentaire: The pivotal verse: “they returned to themselves” (rajaʿū ilā anfusihim). Abraham's argument has forced them into introspection — however brief — and conscience whispers their own wrong. Qushayrī sees here the moment when evidence pierces the veil: faced with the absurd, they recognize for an instant that the wrongdoer is themselves. But the start will be quickly stifled by pride.

mots.racine: ر ج ع / ن ف س
ظ ل م
mots.sens: it is you who are the wrongdoers

versets.ar: ثُمَّ نُكِسُوا عَلَىٰ رُءُوسِهِمْ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَا هَٰؤُلَاءِ يَنطِقُونَ
versets.tr: thumma nukisū ʿalā ruʾūsihim laqad ʿalimta mā hāʾulāʾi yanṭiqūna
versets.trad: Then they were turned upside down: “You know well that these do not speak!”
versets.commentaire: “They were overturned upon their heads” (nukisū): a striking image of the inversion of reason. Scarcely had the truth been glimpsed when pride turns them upside down again. Qushayrī notes their unwitting confession: you know well that they do not speak — which is exactly Abraham's argument, which they turn against him without seeing that it condemns them. To admit the idols are mute, and yet to worship them: such is the blindness of obstinacy.

mots.racine: ن ك س / ر أ س
mots.sens: then they were overturned on their heads

mots.racine: ن ط ق

versets.ar: قَالَ أَفَتَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُكُمْ شَيْئًا وَلَا يَضُرُّكُمْ
versets.tr: qāla a-fa-taʿbudūna min dūni llāhi mā lā yanfaʿukum shayʾan wa-lā yaḍurrukum
versets.trad: He said: “Do you then worship, besides God, what can neither profit nor harm you in anything?
versets.commentaire: Abraham strikes the final blow: to worship what neither profits nor harms is the height of the absurd. Qushayrī makes divinity the decisive criterion: the god is the one from whom creation can proceed. What can do nothing — neither good nor ill — has no claim to worship. The only one worthy of worship is the Source of all power, and no effect can be traced back to the inert idols.

mots.racine: ع ب د
ن ف ع / ض ر ر
mots.def: nafaʿa: to benefit; ḍarra: to harm.

versets.ar: أُفٍّ لَّكُمْ وَلِمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ ۖ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ
versets.tr: uffin lakum wa-li-mā taʿbudūna min dūni llāhi a-fa-lā taʿqilūna
versets.trad: Fie on you and on what you worship besides God! Will you not reason?”
versets.commentaire: “Fie on you!” (uff): Abraham's exclamation mingles indignation and grief. Qushayrī recalls that Abraham first called to awakening, hoping for their return. The reproach “will you not reason?” is the heart of his preaching: he asks not for blind faith, but for the use of intelligence. To worship the inert is to renounce thought; tawḥīd is first of all an act of liberated reason.

mots.racine: أ ف ف
ع ق ل

versets.ar: قَالُوا حَرِّقُوهُ وَانصُرُوا آلِهَتَكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ فَاعِلِينَ
versets.tr: qālū ḥarriqūhu wa-nṣurū ālihatakum in kuntum fāʿilīna
versets.trad: They said: “Burn him and stand by your gods, if you would act!”
versets.commentaire: Out of arguments, they resort to violence. Qushayrī: the argument having turned against them with no answer, susceptibility and partisan fury seized them, and they said: our way is to kill him in the worst manner. Fire is the confession of intellectual defeat: one burns what one cannot refute. “Stand by your gods” — but it is men who must succor the “gods,” the ultimate inversion.

mots.racine: ح ر ق
ن ص ر / أ ل ه

versets.ar: قُلْنَا يَا نَارُ كُونِي بَرْدًا وَسَلَامًا عَلَىٰ إِبْرَاهِيمَ
versets.tr: qulnā yā nāru kūnī bardan wa-salāman ʿalā ibrāhīma
versets.trad: We said: “O fire, be coolness and peace upon Abraham!”
versets.commentaire: The summit of the sūra. Qushayrī meditates: had God preserved him from Nimrod's fire without letting him be cast in, that would have been, outwardly, closer to rescue; but to keep him in the fire without any pain touching him is more perfect in rescue, miracle, and grace. And he relates the sublime word: Gabriel offered him aid in the air — “have you any need? — Of you? No!” And he concludes: when his heart was pure of all but God, the fire became for him coolness and peace; whoever has a sound heart finds safety in the very heart of trial.

mots.racine: ن و ر / ك و ن / ب ر د
س ل م

versets.ar: وَأَرَادُوا بِهِ كَيْدًا فَجَعَلْنَاهُمُ الْأَخْسَرِينَ
versets.tr: wa-arādū bihi kaydan fa-jaʿalnāhumu l-akhsarīna
versets.trad: They wished him a plot, but We made them the greatest losers.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī seals it with a maxim: whoever digs a pit for the friends of God falls into it himself; and whoever is occupied with God, none but God takes charge of avenging him. The persecutors' plot recoils entirely: wishing to destroy Abraham, they destroy only themselves. The saint absorbed in God need not defend himself: God takes charge of his cause.

mots.racine: ر و د / ك ي د
mots.sens: they wished a plot against him

mots.racine: خ س ر
mots.sens: We made them the greatest losers

versets.ar: وَنَجَّيْنَاهُ وَلُوطًا إِلَى الْأَرْضِ الَّتِي بَارَكْنَا فِيهَا لِلْعَالَمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-najjaynāhu wa-lūṭan ilā l-arḍi llatī bāraknā fīhā lil-ʿālamīna
versets.trad: We saved him and Lot to the land We had blessed for all the worlds.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: such is God's custom toward His prophets: when He saves one of them, He joins to his salvation the one who shared his suffering and the trial of his torment. Lot is saved with Abraham, for he had shared his cause. Salvation, with God, does not isolate: it gathers the companions of trial into a single deliverance, toward the land blessed for all the worlds.

mots.racine: ن ج و
أ ر ض / ب ر ك

versets.ar: وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ نَافِلَةً ۖ وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنَا صَالِحِينَ
versets.tr: wa-wahabnā lahu isḥāqa wa-yaʿqūba nāfilatan wa-kullan jaʿalnā ṣāliḥīna
versets.trad: We granted him Isaac and Jacob in addition, and each We made righteous.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He favored him by bringing forth from his loins a progeny worshipping God and remembering Him; for the glories of the sons are merits for the fathers, as the merits of the fathers are an honor for the sons. Isaac and Jacob are given “in addition” (nāfila) — beyond the request. The piety of the offspring extends and crowns the sanctity of the forebear: the line of the righteous is itself a gift.

mots.racine: و ه ب
mots.sens: We granted him Isaac and Jacob

mots.racine: ن ف ل

versets.ar: وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا وَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْرَاتِ وَإِقَامَ الصَّلَاةِ وَإِيتَاءَ الزَّكَاةِ ۖ وَكَانُوا لَنَا عَابِدِينَ
versets.tr: wa-jaʿalnāhum aʾimmatan yahdūna bi-amrinā wa-awḥaynā ilayhim fiʿla l-khayrāti wa-iqāma ṣ-ṣalāti wa-ītāʾa z-zakāti wa-kānū lanā ʿābidīna
versets.trad: We made them leaders (imams) guiding by Our command, and We revealed to them the doing of good, the performing of prayer, and the giving of alms. And they were worshippers of Us.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the imam is the one who goes before the people; the merit of the rank of imamate lies in the gathering in him of the praiseworthy qualities scattered in the community. Whoever does not unite in himself these dispersed virtues does not merit the station of imam. To guide “by Our command”: true direction is not from oneself but from God. The imam gathers in his person what the community holds in pieces — and gives it back as guidance.

mots.racine: أ م م / ه د ي / أ م ر
ع ب د

versets.ar: وَلُوطًا آتَيْنَاهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا وَنَجَّيْنَاهُ مِنَ الْقَرْيَةِ الَّتِي كَانَت تَّعْمَلُ الْخَبَائِثَ ۗ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا قَوْمَ سَوْءٍ فَاسِقِينَ
versets.tr: wa-lūṭan ātaynāhu ḥukman wa-ʿilman wa-najjaynāhu mina l-qaryati llatī kānat taʿmalu l-khabāʾitha innahum kānū qawma sawʾin fāsiqīna
versets.trad: And Lot, to whom We gave judgment and knowledge, and We saved him from the town that committed abominations. They were an evil, depraved people.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He perfected the blessing for him by preserving him from the very trial that struck his people, then by delivering him from them through his going out from among them; thus He distinguished him from them outwardly and inwardly. Lot is doubly saved: preserved from the vice and extracted from the vicious milieu. Knowledge (ʿilm) and judgment (ḥukm) are the light that separates him from the abominations within, before salvation separates him from them without.

mots.racine: ح ك م / ع ل م
mots.sens: We gave him judgment and knowledge

mots.racine: ق ر ي / خ ب ث

versets.ar: وَأَدْخَلْنَاهُ فِي رَحْمَتِنَا ۖ إِنَّهُ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ
versets.tr: wa-adkhalnāhu fī raḥmatinā innahu mina ṣ-ṣāliḥīna
versets.trad: We admitted him into Our mercy. He was indeed of the righteous.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī draws a subtlety from the order of terms: He says that He admitted him into His mercy, then: “he is of the righteous”; without doubt, whoever is brought into His mercy is righteous. And he distinguishes: “We admitted him into Our mercy” is information about union (jamʿ); “he is of the righteous” is information about distinction (farq) — for mercy belongs to the attributes of the Essence, and the servant's righteousness owes in part to his acquisition.

mots.racine: د خ ل / ر ح م
mots.sens: We admitted him into Our mercy

mots.racine: ص ل ح

versets.ar: وَنُوحًا إِذْ نَادَىٰ مِن قَبْلُ فَاسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُ فَنَجَّيْنَاهُ وَأَهْلَهُ مِنَ الْكَرْبِ الْعَظِيمِ
versets.tr: wa-nūḥan idh nādā min qablu fa-stajabnā lahu fa-najjaynāhu wa-ahlahu mina l-karbi l-ʿaẓīmi
versets.trad: And Noah, when he called out before — We answered him and saved him and his family from the great distress.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī recalls Noah's immense trial: he was the longest tried of them; he was beaten, it is said, up to seventy times; the old man would carry his grandson to him, charging him not to heed this elder. Noah endured the suffering patiently, calling them to God. When he despaired of their faith and received the revelation that none more would believe, he prayed against them, and God answered. Noah's patience preceded his deliverance: he called out “before,” long, before he was answered.

mots.racine: ن د و
ك ر ب / ع ظ م

versets.ar: وَنَصَرْنَاهُ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا قَوْمَ سَوْءٍ فَأَغْرَقْنَاهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ
versets.tr: wa-naṣarnāhu mina l-qawmi lladhīna kadhdhabū bi-āyātinā innahum kānū qawma sawʾin fa-aghraqnāhum ajmaʿīna
versets.trad: And We helped him against the people who denied Our signs. They were an evil people, so We drowned them all.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī closes the account of Noah: after the long denial and the prophetic invocation, He annihilated the associationism and drowned its people. The flood is no blind punishment but the answer to an invocation long held back, after the exhaustion of all hope of faith. God's help to His prophet came through the drowning of the deniers: Noah's patience was crowned by God's justice.

mots.racine: ن ص ر / ق و م
mots.sens: We helped him against the people

mots.racine: غ ر ق / ج م ع

versets.ar: وَدَاوُودَ وَسُلَيْمَانَ إِذْ يَحْكُمَانِ فِي الْحَرْثِ إِذْ نَفَشَتْ فِيهِ غَنَمُ الْقَوْمِ وَكُنَّا لِحُكْمِهِمْ شَاهِدِينَ
versets.tr: wa-dāwūda wa-sulaymāna idh yaḥkumāni fī l-ḥarthi idh nafashat fīhi ghanamu l-qawmi wa-kunnā li-ḥukmihim shāhidīna
versets.trad: And David and Solomon, when they judged concerning the field into which the people's sheep had strayed by night — and We were witness to their judgment.
themes: the judgment of David and Solomon
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He associated them in the judgment of prophethood, though between their degrees there was a difference. The verse, he adds, attests the validity of differing interpretive efforts (ijtihād) when they bear on the branches of religion, since God says: “to each We gave judgment and knowledge” — even though Solomon received the particular understanding of the matter. Two sincere judgments, and both praised: such is the breadth of wisdom.

mots.racine: ح ك م / ح ر ث
ن ف ش / غ ن م
mots.sens: the people's sheep grazed in it by night
mots.def: nafasha: to graze at night; ghanam: sheep.

versets.ar: فَفَهَّمْنَاهَا سُلَيْمَانَ ۚ وَكُلًّا آتَيْنَا حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا ۚ وَسَخَّرْنَا مَعَ دَاوُودَ الْجِبَالَ يُسَبِّحْنَ وَالطَّيْرَ ۚ وَكُنَّا فَاعِلِينَ
versets.tr: fa-fahhamnāhā sulaymāna wa-kullan ātaynā ḥukman wa-ʿilman wa-sakhkharnā maʿa dāwūda l-jibāla yusabbiḥna wa-ṭ-ṭayra wa-kunnā fāʿilīna
versets.trad: We gave Solomon understanding of it, and to each We gave judgment and knowledge. We subjected the mountains and the birds to glorify with David. We were the doers.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He distinguished Solomon by this particular understanding, without crediting it to him as He credited him the kingdom — proof that knowledge is worth more than power. And on David: He commanded the mountains and subjected them to assist David in glorification; he would pass, and the flanks of the mountains would answer him in echo, and the birds would accompany him. All nature becomes a choir around the prophet who sings the praises: the cosmos answers the praying heart.

mots.racine: ف ه م
س خ ر / ج ب ل / س ب ح
mots.sens: We subjected the mountains glorifying with David

versets.ar: وَعَلَّمْنَاهُ صَنْعَةَ لَبُوسٍ لَّكُمْ لِتُحْصِنَكُم مِّن بَأْسِكُمْ ۖ فَهَلْ أَنتُمْ شَاكِرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-ʿallamnāhu ṣanʿata labūsin lakum li-tuḥṣinakum min baʾsikum fa-hal antum shākirūna
versets.trad: We taught him the making of coats of mail for you, to protect you from your own violence. Are you then grateful?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: God subjected iron to David and softened it in his hand; he wove coats of mail to guard against arrows in war, and perfected the work, firming the rivets. Then this point: but when the arrows of the decree took aim at him, they struck only his pupil — when he looked, unintending, at the wife of Uriah; and the mail he had learned did not protect him. No armor protects from the shaft of destiny.

mots.racine: ص ن ع / ل ب س
mots.sens: the making of armor for you

mots.racine: ح ص ن / ب أ س
mots.sens: to protect you from your violence

versets.ar: وَلِسُلَيْمَانَ الرِّيحَ عَاصِفَةً تَجْرِي بِأَمْرِهِ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ الَّتِي بَارَكْنَا فِيهَا ۚ وَكُنَّا بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَالِمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-li-sulaymāna r-rīḥa ʿāṣifatan tajrī bi-amrihi ilā l-arḍi llatī bāraknā fīhā wa-kunnā bi-kulli shayʾin ʿālimīna
versets.trad: And to Solomon the stormy wind, running at his command to the land We had blessed. We have knowledge of all things.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: God subjected the wind to him, its going a month, its return a month; but had he wished to add a span to its distance, he could not — to teach him that he depended on the decree. And the admirable tale: one day the wind tilted his carpet slightly; Solomon said to it: be straight! The wind replied: be straight yourself — my tilting is from the tilting of your heart. Power itself, when subjected, reminds the saint that all depends on inner rectitude.

mots.racine: ر و ح / ع ص ف / ج ر ي
mots.sens: the stormy wind running at his command

mots.racine: ع ل م

versets.ar: وَمِنَ الشَّيَاطِينِ مَن يَغُوصُونَ لَهُ وَيَعْمَلُونَ عَمَلًا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ وَكُنَّا لَهُمْ حَافِظِينَ
versets.tr: wa-mina sh-shayāṭīni man yaghūṣūna lahu wa-yaʿmalūna ʿamalan dūna dhālika wa-kunnā lahum ḥāfiẓīna
versets.trad: And of the devils were some who dived for him and did other works besides, while We kept watch over them.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī follows on to Solomon's end, a lesson on the fragility of power: this lasted, in truth, only a few days; then the angel of death came to claim his soul as he stood leaning on his staff; he remained thus, and the jinn knew nothing, until the creature of the earth gnawed the staff: then Solomon collapsed, and the devils knew of his death. He who ruled the devils was ruled by death: no empire stands before it.

mots.racine: غ و ص
ح ف ظ

versets.ar: وَأَيُّوبَ إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ الضُّرُّ وَأَنتَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-ayyūba idh nādā rabbahu annī massaniya ḍ-ḍurru wa-anta arḥamu r-rāḥimīna
versets.trad: And Job, when he called upon his Lord: “Harm has touched me, and You are the most Merciful of the merciful!”
versets.commentaire: A sublime page of Qushayrī. He notes first the adab: he did not say “have mercy on me,” but kept the courtesy of address, saying: “You, the most Merciful of the merciful.” And he offers overwhelming readings: it was not a complaint but a praise: “harm has touched me” — this harm with which You favor Your friends; had You not been the most Merciful, You would not have graced me with it. More poignant still: it was not Job who complained of the trial, it was the trial that could not bear his company and cried out, at its limit, beneath his patience.

mots.racine: م س س / ض ر ر
ر ح م

versets.ar: فَاسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُ فَكَشَفْنَا مَا بِهِ مِن ضُرٍّ ۖ وَآتَيْنَاهُ أَهْلَهُ وَمِثْلَهُم مَّعَهُمْ رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا وَذِكْرَىٰ لِلْعَابِدِينَ
versets.tr: fa-stajabnā lahu fa-kashafnā mā bihi min ḍurrin wa-ātaynāhu ahlahu wa-mithlahum maʿahum raḥmatan min ʿindinā wa-dhikrā lil-ʿābidīna
versets.trad: We answered him and removed the harm that was upon him, and We gave him his family and the like of them with them, as a mercy from Us and a reminder for the worshippers.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī notes the swiftness of the response: the “fa” of “We answered him” implies the immediate sequel: as if He said: We healed him at once. As if He said: O Job, had you asked for healing before this, We would have answered you. And he gives a dizzying reading: the affliction (ḍurr) he complained of was that a remnant still remained of him, and his trial was in that remnant; when he was wholly delivered from it, the trial ceased — then trial and rescue are equalized, existence and loss. The doubled gift rewards pure patience.

mots.racine: ك ش ف / ض ر ر
mots.sens: We removed the harm afflicting him

mots.racine: ر ح م / ذ ك ر
mots.sens: mercy from Us and a reminder

versets.ar: وَإِسْمَاعِيلَ وَإِدْرِيسَ وَذَا الْكِفْلِ ۖ كُلٌّ مِّنَ الصَّابِرِينَ
versets.tr: wa-ismāʿīla wa-idrīsa wa-dhā l-kifli kullun mina ṣ-ṣābirīna
versets.trad: And Ishmael, and Idris (Enoch), and Dhū al-Kifl — all were among the patient.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: that is: remember these prophets; then He says: “all were among the patient.” Patience (ṣabr) is the common thread linking Ishmael, Idris, and Dhū al-Kifl to Job. It is the cardinal virtue of the tried prophets: not mere endurance, but the firmness of heart beneath the decree. To name their patience is to make it the model offered to the believers.

mots.racine: ص ب ر

versets.ar: وَأَدْخَلْنَاهُمْ فِي رَحْمَتِنَا ۖ إِنَّهُم مِّنَ الصَّالِحِينَ
versets.tr: wa-adkhalnāhum fī raḥmatinā innahum mina ṣ-ṣāliḥīna
versets.trad: We admitted them into Our mercy; they were of the righteous.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī distinguishes here the ruling and the deeper meaning: the ruling is their patience and their righteousness; the meaning is that He admitted them into His mercy. In other words: righteousness is their apparent acquisition, but the entrance into mercy is God's gratuitous gift. Sanctity holds from both: the servant's patient effort and the sovereign grace that receives him.

mots.racine: د خ ل / ر ح م
mots.sens: We admitted them into Our mercy

mots.racine: ص ل ح

versets.ar: وَذَا النُّونِ إِذ ذَّهَبَ مُغَاضِبًا فَظَنَّ أَن لَّن نَّقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ فَنَادَىٰ فِي الظُّلُمَاتِ أَن لَّا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا أَنتَ سُبْحَانَكَ إِنِّي كُنتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-dhā n-nūni idh dhahaba mughāḍiban fa-ẓanna an lan naqdira ʿalayhi fa-nādā fī ẓ-ẓulumāti an lā ilāha illā anta subḥānaka innī kuntu mina ẓ-ẓālimīna
versets.trad: And Dhū al-Nūn (Jonah), when he went off angry, thinking We had no power over him. Then he called out in the darkness: “There is no god but You; glory be to You! I was indeed among the wrongdoers.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reads “lan naqdira ʿalayhi” not as “We shall have no power” but: “We shall not straiten upon him” the belly of the fish — from qadara in the sense of “to measure, to restrict.” And on the darknesses: darkness of night, darkness of sea, darkness of the fish's belly — such is the literal interpretation; but it may be that the darknesses are what grew obscure to him of his moment and what became confused of his state. Jonah's cry from the depths of the abyss — “no god but You” — is the prayer of the shipwrecked who keeps only the One. And Qushayrī recalls, tenderly: he kept the fish's company a few days, and until the Resurrection he is called Dhū al-Nūn — God does not efface the bond; what then of the servant who has served Him seventy years?

mots.racine: ذ ه ب / غ ض ب
ن د و / ظ ل م
mots.sens: he called out in the darknesses

mots.racine: أ ل ه / س ب ح
mots.sens: no god but You, glory to You

versets.ar: فَاسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُ وَنَجَّيْنَاهُ مِنَ الْغَمِّ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُنجِي الْمُؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: fa-stajabnā lahu wa-najjaynāhu mina l-ghammi wa-kadhālika nunjī l-muʾminīna
versets.trad: We answered him and saved him from the distress. Thus do We save the believers.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We answered him, though he had not even formed a request — he had said only: “no god but You, glory to You, I was among the wrongdoers”; and he acknowledged his wrong only while seeking forgiveness for it. Then the universal promise: every believer who, struck by anguish or faced with a grave matter, says as Dhū al-Nūn said, We deliver him as We delivered Dhū al-Nūn. The pure attestation, without petition, suffices: to acknowledge the One and confess one's fault is already deliverance.

mots.racine: ن ج و / غ م م
mots.sens: We saved him from the anguish

mots.racine: ن ج و / أ م ن

versets.ar: وَزَكَرِيَّا إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُ رَبِّ لَا تَذَرْنِي فَرْدًا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ الْوَارِثِينَ
versets.tr: wa-zakariyyā idh nādā rabbahu rabbi lā tadharnī fardan wa-anta khayru l-wārithīna
versets.trad: And Zachariah, when he called upon his Lord: “My Lord, do not leave me alone! You are the best of inheritors.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: he asked for the child, but he asked for him only to have a help in worshipping his Lord, that he might take his place in prophethood, and that the blessing of the message not be cut off from his house. He did not, then, ask for a son for his own sake but for God's right. And the adab of “You are the best of inheritors”: even in asking, Zachariah remits everything to God, knowing that the true posterity is in Him.

mots.racine: و ذ ر / ف ر د
و ر ث
mots.sens: You are the best of inheritors

versets.ar: فَاسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُ وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ يَحْيَىٰ وَأَصْلَحْنَا لَهُ زَوْجَهُ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا يُسَارِعُونَ فِي الْخَيْرَاتِ وَيَدْعُونَنَا رَغَبًا وَرَهَبًا ۖ وَكَانُوا لَنَا خَاشِعِينَ
versets.tr: fa-stajabnā lahu wa-wahabnā lahu yaḥyā wa-aṣlaḥnā lahu zawjahu innahum kānū yusāriʿūna fī l-khayrāti wa-yadʿūnanā raghaban wa-rahaban wa-kānū lanā khāshiʿīna
versets.trad: We answered him and granted him John (Yaḥyā), and restored to him his wife's fertility. They hastened to good deeds and called upon Us in hope and in fear, and were humble before Us.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: he was named Yaḥyā (he lives) because through him his mother's barrenness came back to life. And “We restored to him his wife”: that the grace of the child might be for them all, and that Zachariah not rejoice alone, out of regard for the right of her companionship. And on “in hope and fear”: the believer never leaves one of the two states — hope or fear; without hope it would be despair, and despair is unbelief; without fear it would be false security, and security is unbelief. Humility (khushūʿ) is the shudder of the heart beneath the Lord's gaze.

mots.racine: و ه ب / ح ي ي
د ع و / ر غ ب / ر ه ب
mots.sens: they called upon Us in hope and fear

versets.ar: وَالَّتِي أَحْصَنَتْ فَرْجَهَا فَنَفَخْنَا فِيهَا مِن رُّوحِنَا وَجَعَلْنَاهَا وَابْنَهَا آيَةً لِّلْعَالَمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-llatī aḥṣanat farjahā fa-nafakhnā fīhā min rūḥinā wa-jaʿalnāhā wa-bnahā āyatan lil-ʿālamīna
versets.trad: And she who guarded her chastity — We breathed into her of Our Spirit, and made her and her son a sign for the worlds.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He washed her of all suspicion of unchastity and of every blameworthy taint. And on the inbreathing: the breath was from Gabriel, but as it was by God's command, the attribution to God is valid. As for the “sign” in the singular for the two: because their affair was but a single miracle and a single indication; and whoever considers their case, placing the gaze as it should be, is guided. Mary and her son are a single sign for all the worlds — the virginal motherhood, a miracle that refutes both suspicion and pretension.

mots.racine: ح ص ن / ف ر ج
ن ف خ / ر و ح
